content:
6 min read
Just now

--

1. What It Is
“Lost in the Middle” describes a consistent failure pattern in large language models: when a model is given a long context containing multiple pieces of information, it retrieves and uses information located at the beginning and end of that context far more reliably than information buried in the middle.
Plotted as accuracy vs. position of the relevant fact in the context window, the curve looks like a U-shape (sometimes called the “U-curve” or “primacy-recency effect” in LLMs):
Accuracy
^
| * *
| * *
| * *
| * *
| * *
| * * * * * *
+--------------------------------------------> Position of relevant info
Start End
• Primacy effect: info near the start of the prompt is well-attended.

• Recency effect: info near the end of the prompt is well-attended.

• Middle degradation: info placed in the middle third (roughly) of a long context is significantly more likely to be ignored, hallucinated over, or answered incorrectly — even though it is technically “within” the context window and the model never runs out of tokens to process it.
This was formally characterized in the paper “Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts” (Liu et al., Stanford/UC Berkeley/Samaya AI), which tested models on multi-document question answering and key-value retrieval tasks with controlled placement of the answer-bearing document.
2. Why It Happens
There isn’t one single agreed-upon root cause, but the leading explanations are:
a. Positional bias baked in by training data and objective
Causal (decoder-only) language models are trained to predict the next token given everything before it. During pretraining and fine-tuning, tokens near sequence boundaries are statistically more informative for next-token prediction than tokens deep inside a long span, so the model’s attention patterns naturally develop a bias toward the edges of whatever sequence it’s processing.
b. Attention dilution over long sequences
Self-attention scores are normalized (softmax) across all tokens in context. As sequence length grows, attention mass gets spread thinner across more tokens, and middle tokens compete with a larger pool of neighbors for attention weight — diluting their effective signal relative to tokens near clear boundaries (start/end) that serve as natural anchors.
c. Positional encoding artifacts
Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) and similar relative-position schemes decay in reliability as relative distance grows. Recent-token relationships (small relative distance) are encoded more precisely than long-range relationships, which structurally favors recency over “middle” retrieval, especially past the effective context length the model was actually trained on (as opposed to the maximum context length it technically accepts).
d. Training context length mismatch
Many models are trained predominantly on shorter sequences and then have context length “extended” afterward (via techniques like position interpolation, NTK-aware scaling, or long-context fine-tuning on a smaller amount of long data). The model’s true competence at using arbitrary middle positions can lag behind its nominal context window size.
e. Serial-position-like effects (analogy to human memory)
The pattern is reminiscent of the serial position effect in human psychology (primacy/recency in list recall). While the underlying mechanism in transformers is architectural rather than cognitive, the resulting curve shape is strikingly similar, which is part of why the phenomenon is intuitive to reason about.
3. Where It Shows Up in Practice

• Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG): if a RAG pipeline retrieves 10 chunks and stuffs them all into the prompt, the single chunk containing the actual answer may be ignored if it lands in position 5 or 6 rather than position 1 or 10.

• Long document QA / summarization: facts stated in the middle of a long contract, research paper, or transcript are more likely to be missed than facts in the introduction or conclusion.

• Multi-turn agent memory: when conversation history or tool-call logs are concatenated into a single long context, earlier “middle” turns can be effectively forgotten even though they’re technically still in-window.

• Tool-use / multi-document synthesis agents: agentic pipelines that concatenate many tool outputs before a final reasoning step are especially exposed, since the number of “middle” positions grows with the number of tool calls.

• Code repositories in context: when large codebases are dumped into context (e.g., whole-repo prompts), functions or files in the middle of the concatenation are more likely to be under-weighted during reasoning about cross-file logic.
This is directly relevant to multi-tenant agentic platforms and RAG-style systems, where prompt construction order is a design choice, not an accident.
4. Mitigation Strategies
No fix eliminates the effect entirely, but several engineering strategies reduce its impact:
a. Reorder by relevance, not retrieval order
Place the most relevant / highest-confidence documents or facts first and last, not in whatever order they were retrieved. Some RAG frameworks explicitly implement a “reorder” step for this reason (e.g., placing the top-ranked chunk both near the start and duplicating a summary near the end).
b. Reduce context size / better retrieval precision
Fewer, higher-precision chunks reduce the number of “middle” positions altogether. Aggressive reranking (e.g., cross-encoder rerankers) before insertion into the prompt tends to help more than simply increasing the number of retrieved chunks.
c. Chunking and summarization
Compress or summarize less-critical middle content, and keep the most decision-relevant material verbatim, near the boundaries.
d. Hierarchical / map-reduce prompting
Instead of one giant context, split into smaller sub-contexts, get an answer or extraction from each independently (each benefiting from the strong start/end recall region), and combine results in a final aggregation step.
e. Explicit position markers and instructions
Some practitioners insert explicit markers (“Document 3 of 10: …”) and instruct the model to review each document individually before answering, which measurably helps some models attend more evenly.
f. Repetition / duplication of critical facts
Duplicating a critical fact near the end of the context (in addition to its natural position) increases the odds it survives whatever positional attenuation occurs in the middle — at the cost of extra tokens.
g. Long-context fine-tuning and architecture choices
Some newer models are explicitly fine-tuned with training examples that require middle-position retrieval, and architectural changes (e.g., improved position encodings, attention sinks, or retrieval-augmented attention) are active areas of research aimed at flattening the U-curve.
h. Empirical testing over assumption
Since the severity of the effect varies significantly across model families, versions, and context lengths, the most reliable mitigation is to benchmark your own pipeline — construct a controlled needle-in-a-haystack style test with your actual document types and context lengths, and measure retrieval accuracy as a function of position before assuming a fix works.
5. How to Test for It Yourself (Needle-in-a-Haystack Style)
A minimal evaluation setup:

• Take a long, low-information “haystack” document (or concatenate several unrelated documents).

• Insert a distinct, unambiguous fact (“needle”) — e.g., a unique fake fact or ID — at a controlled position (e.g., 0%, 10%, 25%, 50%, 75%, 90%, 100% of the way through).

• Ask a question whose answer requires the needle.

• Repeat across many positions and multiple random needles/questions.

• Plot accuracy (or a partial-credit similarity score) against needle position.

• Repeat at multiple total context lengths to see how the effect changes as context grows.
This kind of test is directly reusable for evaluating any RAG or agentic pipeline you’re building — it’s a cheap, high-signal diagnostic before trusting a system with long-context retrieval.
6. Key Takeaway
The “Lost in the Middle” problem is a structural property of how current transformer-based LLMs attend to long contexts, not a bug specific to one model. Context window size is not the same as effective context utilization — a model that “accepts” 128K or 1M tokens can still silently under-use information placed in the middle of that window. Prompt construction order, retrieval ranking, and chunking strategy are therefore first-class design decisions in any long-context or RAG system, not implementation details.

Reference: Liu, N. F., et al. “Lost in the Middle: How Language Models Use Long Contexts.” Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics (TACL), 2023/2024.
